data.title: 2026 S&P 500 Outlook: A Multiple Compression, In Spite Of Earnings Growth
data.description:
For relatively young investors, 2025 would go down in history as a year of average returns in the equity market. The index is up by almost 18% year-to-date and that's roughly in-line to what we have gotten used to since 2019.

On a longer-term basis, however, 2025 would be classified as an extraordinary period for equities. On the graph below, we have the 5-year average annual returns of the S&P 500 and for the past 5-year period the index has delivered a return of 15% on average.

As we can see, this is within the norms of recent history, but on a long-term basis, such returns did not persist for long. During the first decade of this millennium, for example, the average 5-year rolling return of the S&P 500 was gravitating within the range of 0% to 5% - something that is thought as impossible within the current market conditions.

There are many interpretations for the graph above looks like that and whether or not we are in a "new normal" for the markets where recessions are brief and the returns never disappoint.

The fact of the matter is that it is a perfect illustration why we should not take recent performance as a given and why as investors we should remain skeptical every time someone uses recent history as a predictor for the future.

With all that in mind, we are ready to take a fresh look at what the most likely scenario for the S&P 500 in 2026 looks like.

The Current Consensus

Before we go into some of the specifics about the 2026 outlook, we should first take a closer look at what the current consensus is. First, we should establish what the current assumptions behind the overall equity market are and then bring some critical thinking whether or not these are reasonable for long-term holders.

At present, just a few days before we enter 2026, the consensus analysts' estimate is for the S&P 500 earnings to grow at a rate of 15% in calendar year 2026.

Analysts expect the S&P 500 to report double-digit earnings growth for the 3rd straight year in CY 2026. The estimated (year-over-year) earnings growth rate for CY 2026 is 15.0%, which is above the trailing 10-year average (annual) earnings growth rate of 8.6% (2015 – 2024).

Source: Factset

Note that the 15% growth is well above the average for the past 10-year period, but more importantly it is almost twice as high as the expected Q4 2025 earnings growth of 8.3%. As expected, all eyes are on the technology sector and given the sector's record-high weight within the S&P 500, it has become the only game in town for the equity market in 2026. The materials sector comes in second for expected earnings growth, but its weight within the index is negligible.

On top of the high revenue growth in 2026, the net profit margin of S&P 500 companies is expected to be around 13% - another record high.

From a narrative point of view, it is quite easy to justify these numbers just by looking at what companies like Nvidia (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOGL), Oracle (ORCL) etc. are achieving in terms of earnings growth and margins.

The problem, however, is that other issues continue to mount:

  • Firstly, it is the problem with the S&P 500 extreme levels of concentration into just a few companies. This is something that I covered in further detail back in September.
  • Secondly, there's the issue with valuations which in 2025 went from slightly concerning to a major red flag.
  • Lastly, it is the systemic risk related to an upcoming change of the existing monetary system and heightened geopolitical tensions.

For a further explanation of the first issue you can follow the link above. The second and the third one I will cover down below.

A Rare Sight For The S&P 500

As a starting point, even if one is to assume that the 15% earnings growth in 2026 is achievable and that it will not fall-off a cliff in the following years, then the index still trades at a forward earnings multiple that is well-above the historical one standard deviation. The two other periods when we had that were in the early 2000s (prior to the dot-com bubble) and in 2020.

These nose-bleed valuation levels should also be reviewed through the scope of risk, and volatility is the most widely used metric of total risk in the equity market. In that regard, we had a significant spike in volatility earlier in 2025 when the S&P 500 fell sharply in March and early April.

On a historical basis, such sharp movements in volatility usually happen during more pronounced bear markets when the cyclically adjusted price-to-earnings ratio (CAPE) plummets. These periods are marked with a red arrow on the graph below.

The year 2020 might look like an exception, but in the first half of the year we saw the CAPE ratio falling from above 30 to around 25 in a matter of days.

One could argue that the same has happened in 2025, but as we can see on the graph below the actual figures were quite different. The ratio's lowest point was still well-above 30 and it is now approaching 40.

These metrics are truly unprecedented and the brief bear market we had in March and April of 2025 clearly illustrates the fragility of investor confidence, which coupled with the record-high valuation of the S&P 500 pose significant downside risks for 2026.

Yet another clear sign of the risks lurking beneath the surface is the price of gold, which for some reason investors now tend to ignore.

The High Systemic Risk

On a year-to-date basis, the price of gold has gone up by more than 50% and that's something that goes unnoticed by many. Not the fact that anyone who has been holding gold as an addition to his/her portfolio has made good returns this year, but the underlying reason why the precious metal has skyrocketed in price.

The reason why gold is up so much in 2025 and in the few years prior is solely due to the overall risk for the monetary system that investors are now beginning to price-in. I have covered all that in a lot of detail in a thought piece on the SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) from early 2021.

In a nutshell, the current period for gold is only comparable to years when investors started to realize that a change in the monetary system is imminent or in periods following the adoption of a new system when risk and uncertainty around it have peaked.

Back to our present day, the extraordinary reaction of precious metals in 2025 should be a major warning sign for equity investors. It is a sign that equity investors might not be properly accounting for risks as we enter 2026 and this could have grave consequences for returns.

The skyrocketing gold to oil ratio is quite telling about the highly supportive commodity prices which are at odds with the brewing monetary risks.

The plan of the Trump Administration to lower oil prices in order to support the economy seem to be working well which in combination with the artificial intelligence boom in 2025 have been more than enough to offset the aforementioned risks associated with the price of gold. But as we enter the new year, the confidence on Main Street remains sluggish, with the Leading Economic Index continuing to decline year-on-year and consumer confidence falling for a fifth consecutive month in December.

The 2026 Outlook

Based on everything so far it appears highly unlikely that 2026 will be a very rewarding year for equity investors. The S&P 500 reached new all-time highs in 2025 on the back of the AI boom and the highly supportive macroeconomic conditions. But as the year progressed two major risks became apparent:

  • Firstly, it is the record high valuation multiples in the equity market and expectations about earnings growth in 2026 that will be hard to justify;
  • And secondly, the price of gold has sent a strong signal that buyers of the S&P 500 at current levels are taking unnecessary risks.

With that in mind, the forward price/earnings multiple of the index would most likely fall below one standard deviation line we saw above. In other words, the best-case scenario for the S&P 500 is to experience a multiple compression in 2026 to around 20 times forward earnings.

At present, the S&P 500 earnings for 2025 are estimated to be around $240. Assuming the aforementioned 15% growth in 2026 becomes a reality and is then sustained for 2027 then this would give us earnings of roughly $320. At 20 times forward multiple this results in the S&P 500 being at $6,380 at the end of 2026 or an 8% drop from current levels.

Although I expressed some skepticism about these earnings growth estimates and especially when it comes to their sustainability in 2027, the highly supportive macroeconomic conditions in combination with the anticipation for significant interest rate cuts next year could make that a reality. The risks mentioned above, however, could make investors a bit more cautious in the coming year (assuming a systemic event is avoided) which in my view makes a multiple compression the most likely scenario for next year.

data.title: Danone: Defensive, Resilient... And Still Just A Hold
data.description:
Danone S.A. (DANOY) is a brand that I, and other Brazilians, know relatively well. The brand is very strong here in Brazil, positioned slightly more premium, and has also marked childhoods with products such as Danoninho (or Danonino in other countries). This experience is not unique to Brazil; in fact, Danone is a strong brand in many parts of the world. In addition, the company is not just yogurt, which is the part I was most aware of, but it expands to other consumer staples products, including some very interesting possibilities.

But despite this consistency of the company and very well-established brands, I don't think Danone is a good investment right now, so I give it a Hold, signaling that I expect a better entry point to change to a buy rating. Among the main reasons, I highlight the cyclical/structural problems for consumer staples, as well as the valuation.

Danone S.A.: The Part I Like

There are a few points that catch my attention in Danone's thesis. The first point is that it is a company that is already well established, not only in terms of brands as mentioned, but also in distribution and global penetration, and this is a clear moat that helps maintain leadership and also explore new products. For example, in the table below, note how there is a well-diversified spread across different geographies, and also that there is not such a heavy dependence on just one category, such as Essential Dairy. This means that challenges such as more intense competition in respective industries or geographies do not affect it as much. Another point that I like, but which ends up being a double-edged sword, is that Danone is a company with greater European exposure, that is, it is a form of exposure to a very resilient currency and removes some of the dependence on the USD in the portfolio. Also, note in the table that growth has been quite consistent lately, driven mainly by volume and price.

The company's profile is very defensive, and its historical financials reflect this, both positively and negatively. On the positive side, the company is very consistent and predictable, in a way. But on the negative side, because it is a very consolidated company, we don't see very encouraging numbers. The revenue CAGR is ~2% over the last 10 years, while for net income we are already talking about a CAGR of 7%, meaning that even though the top line is not growing as much, the company has proven that it can capture efficiency through operational leverage, mix, and the like.

A third point is that, in addition to being a defensive company with very solid financials and, as a result, able to generate cash and distribute it to shareholders via dividends, there are the possibilities. The company has the expertise to ride new trends to remain relevant and also unlock more growth. A clear example for me is YoPro, a very practical protein drink that has helped revenue growth in Latin America. In other words, the company saw the trend toward protein drinks, people seeking a healthier and at the same time more practical lifestyle, and is able to execute this well.

Danone’s Problems: Valuation and... The Rest

Well, the problem is that all of the above is very well priced by the market. Famous brands, defensive profile, strong currency, all of this is being priced by the market at a P/E of 19x for fiscal year 2. It may seem a little low for such quality, but remember, we are talking about a company that is growing at low single digits and is unlikely to surprise positively again, i.e., it is an earnings yield of ~5% that is growing at a slow pace, nothing that really stands out.

This valuation is even worse when we note that even when compared to even more consolidated conglomerates such as Nestlé (NSRGY), Danone is trading at a slight premium, even with similar growth. Compared to other players in the industry, the valuation is also not that good. Mondelez (MDLZ) and Kraft Heinz (KHC) are cheaper, and even Hershey's (HSY) has a similar estimated P/E for the coming years.

In other words, it is not exactly the valuation that is the big problem, but rather the valuation in relation to other things. Growth is nothing to write home about, and neither are the existing risks.

Personally, I have been somewhat wary lately of companies with a profile similar to Danone. It seems that this staples market is becoming increasingly competitive. Very incumbent brands, such as those of influencers, manage to achieve high penetration given the low cost of marketing and/or strong organic marketing on the internet, premium brands with more established differentials for some niches are beginning to gain traction, and also, for the more price-sensitive consumer, several supermarket proprietary brands are beginning to offer good value. In other words, it is becoming increasingly difficult for companies like Danone to position themselves clearly. It is not a luxury good where you are paying a high price to consume the top brand, but at the same time, you are not paying the cheapest price.

I don't think this creates a significant risk of disruption or bankruptcy for Danone, but it makes the growth scenario more uncertain. It is increasingly necessary for the company to invest in new products, marketing, and reinvent itself to compete in this market, and it is also increasingly difficult to envision sustainable high growth, such as high single digits for several years in a row.

The Bottom Line

Even though I find it an interesting company, especially for those who want exposure to a European company with a defensive profile, I don't think Danone is an interesting choice right now. There is no particularly interesting growth, no obvious triggers, and no particularly attractive valuation. Therefore, my rating is a Hold for DANOY stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: BLOX Vs. LFGY: This Crypto Income ETF Is Beating Bitcoin While Others Erode
data.description:
Investment thesis

This is the continuation of my series comparing funds with similar management structures and investment strategies. This time, we are looking at the Nicholas Crypto Income ETF (BLOX) and the YieldMax Crypto Industry & Tech Portfolio Option Income ETF (LFGY), which are notable for their use of option strategies. Since the current market for Bitcoin and cryptocurrency stocks is trending downward, the selection of assets that can demonstrate positive results regardless of the market cycle is important. Taking the last six months as an example, the total return of BLOX is +15.51%, whereas the spot iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT) is -14.62%. With this in mind, it is of fundamental interest to me to understand the "secret" of BLOX's success and determine whether LFGY can repeat its results or, even better, outperform them. My fundamental analysis has led me to be skeptical about LFGY, so my recommendation for this fund is to "Hold," whereas for BLOX it is to "Buy." This article provides answers to all questions surrounding these funds.

Fundamental similarities and differences between BLOX and LFGY

The BLOX and LFGY funds are structurally similar exchange-traded funds focused on the development of the crypto industry. If BLOX is characterized by combining exposure to cryptocurrencies (through shares of cryptocurrency companies and ETFs with Bitcoin as the underlying asset) and option strategies to generate high returns, then LGFY is similar in its operating principle, except that it does not include spot ETH-BTC ETFs in its portfolio. In its place, YieldMax's portfolio managers work exclusively with shares of cryptocurrency companies (though not owning them directly). There is a similarity between these funds in their use of options strategies. For this reason, BLOX and LFGY's results are highly volatile and sensitive to the market cycle in the cryptocurrency market.

In addition, such funds are essentially designed for a specific target audience of income investors who are willing to accept increased volatility and capital erosion risks in exchange for high returns. The sensitivity of fund performance to volatility reflects their ability to generate high returns. When the price of assets on the cryptocurrency market is more unstable, it becomes more expensive to buy options that portfolio managers sell. As a result, higher premiums are generated. At the same time, this feature limits growth. In other words, if, let's say, Bitcoin prices start to rise again quickly, the funds will sacrifice some of their profits because of the covered call strategy.

But there are also differences, mostly related to how they build their portfolios. Check out BLOX, it's got a really balanced structure for a fund dealing with crypto assets. The main chunk is in three spot Bitcoin ETFs, making up 29.11%. This is followed by various assets that are directly or indirectly related to the development of the crypto industry. It even includes shares of companies that belong to the Mag-7. Nvidia, for example, accounts for 3.72% of the BLOX portfolio. Combining such different types of assets makes the fund's portfolio balanced, partially diversified, and included in the overall development of the crypto industry.

LFGY previously focused exclusively on cryptocurrency industry stocks. It mainly included shares of various miners, cryptocurrency treasury holders, and crypto exchanges. But today, it has a bunch of other assets, such as Nvidia shares and the Ethereum spot ETF (iShares Ethereum Trust ETF). Over time, these additions make the LFGY portfolio more balanced and diversified. The majority of the portfolio (11.51%) is made up of options expiring on February 20, 2026. These are long-term options on the underlying asset IBIT with a strike price of $10.01. The portfolios of the funds under review are quite similar in terms of their high concentration among the top 10 assets (56.2% for BLOX and 52.68% for LFGY).

The other difference between BLOX and LFGY lies in their income generation mechanics. Portfolio managers at BLOX employ a combination of selling call options and using put spreads. They do this depending on the market situation, using a modified version of the covered call strategy. The LFGY follows a synthetic scheme, because instead of directly owning shares in selected companies, the portfolio managers create a synthetic long position through options, by selling calls over them. As a result of this strategy, LFGY is characterized by capital erosion.

The chart below shows the changes in total returns over the last 6 months (since BLOX began operating on June 16, 2025). The result is that BLOX's total return is 15.51%, whereas LFGY's is a loss of 2.66%. Such results only prove the hypothesis about the huge difference between BLOX and LFGY in terms of investment strategy, portfolio formation approach, and yield generation mechanics.

Comparative table of BLOX and LFGY characteristics

The table below compares the main criteria that distinguish BLOX from LFGY. Many criteria favor BLOX. Firstly, its more structured approach to asset selection and its combined options strategy. Although the risk concentration is more manageable, its asset structure is as high as that of LFGY. Several other metrics have similar values, and the dividend yield does not yet fully reflect the full potential of BLOX, the full extent of which will be determined after the first full year of dividend payments. For this reason, its performance is significantly lower. But for me, the overall return is still more important in assessing the attractiveness of these ETFs, where BLOX has a clear advantage.

Criteria

BLOX

LFGY

Key components

BTC/ETH ETF + Stocks

Crypto Stocks (Basket)

Income Source

Stock Options and ETFs

Covered calls

Counterparty Risk

Moderate (diversification by class)

High (dependence on MSTR and COIN)

Beta to Bitcoin

~0.7 – 0.9

~1.1 – 1.4

Number of Holdings

Assets in Top 10

Weekly

Total Return, 1М
