Total Return, 6М

15.51%

-2.66%

Risks of investing in crypto industry ETFs during Bitcoin correction

In one of my recent articles on IBIT, based on technical analysis, I downgraded Bitcoin from "Buy" to "Hold." The weekly chart shows a break below the lower boundary of the global uptrend channel, indicating a potential correction to the key support zone ($74,400–$78,200). This signal is also confirmed by the daily chart analysis, where the medium-term downward trend channel signals a decline to the above-mentioned price zone. Ultimately, it is likely that we will see a continuation of Bitcoin's corrective decline to 20%.

Suppose this decline does occur. How will ETFs such as BLOX and LFGY be affected?

For BLOX, I think the expected drop in NAV will be somewhere between 18% and 25%. Those parts of the fund's portfolio that are made up of spot Bitcoin ETFs, such as IBIT, would fall by exactly the same 20%. On the other hand, shares of crypto companies, including miners and exchanges, would lose somewhere between 30% and 40%. They have a beta of 1.5x to 2.0x relative to Bitcoin volatility. Still, using put spreads and rebalancing the investment portfolio would likely soften the blow. Volatility premiums from selling call options will offset up to 3% of the losses caused by the decline in the value of assets in the portfolio. In the end, fund performance may be comparable to the decline in Bitcoin prices.

For LFGY, I expect NAV to drop somewhere between 35% and 45%. The fund's portfolio consists of shares in cryptocurrency companies, sometimes with a beta to Bitcoin volatility even higher than 2.0x. As an example, if Bitcoin's price drops by 1%, the price of MARA drops by 2.5%. Because the fund doesn't directly own the stocks but instead has a synthetic position through options, a moment of market panic can lead to a faster loss in value due to changes in the Greeks. Of course, selling call options partially offsets the loss with the premium received. However, this is likely to be no more than 5%. As with all YieldMax funds, the LFGY is characterized by significant capital erosion when prices collapse, due to which it will be much more difficult to restore the previous value of the shares. This is because selling calls leads to a reduction in potential profits from strong price growth. As a result, the drawdown will be deeper when investing in LFGY. Even though investors will receive higher dividends, capital erosion will be very strong, negatively affecting total returns.

Conclusion

The results of the comparative analysis between BLOX and LFGY reveal fundamental differences between them, as reflected in the actual performance of the funds. If I had to pick between these two assets, I would go with BLOX, as it has seen positive total return growth over the past six months, whereas LFGY is still in the red. An interesting fact is that if we look at the results of the Bitcoin spot ETF, IBIT, we see that its performance over the same period is even worse (-14.62%). It signals to investors that BLOX portfolio managers, through their options strategy, can offset losses from falling cryptocurrency and cryptocurrency stock prices by using a combination of selling call options and using put spreads. That is why my recommendation for BLOX is "Buy," whereas for LFGY it is "Hold."

data.title: TSMC: Why 2026 Will Be Even Bigger
data.description:
I'm Extending My "Buy" on TSMC To 2026

I don't cover Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) aka TSMC as frequently as I cover similar-sized tech stocks, with my only research piece dating back to December 2024. I had a Buy rating on TSMC at the time because I saw the continuation of "booming demand across multiple areas such as AI and HPC." The reality exceeded even my bullish expectations. TSMC enjoyed a massive inflow of new investors as many people suddenly realized that the company's scale helps it retain and even grow its market share in a very promising niche. As a result, TSM stock exceeded the S&P 500's (SP500) return in 2025.

I believe AI-driven growth in HPC is still in the very early stages, and that TSMC's planned capacity expansion should bring in an earnings acceleration ahead. There are a lot of bullish catalysts (like the ramp of 2nm/1.6nm tech, Foundry 2.0, and the paradoxical benefit of U.S.-China trade tensions) that can unlock value for TSMC's investors in 2026, and if at least some of the catalysts materialize, the current valuation would look like a steal. That's why I leave my Buy rating with no change.

Why Do I Think So?

In Q3, TSM showed one of the biggest double-beatings, with their top line and bottom line beating the consensus estimates by 2.32% and 11.23%, respectively. The revenue increased by 40.8% YoY, which came above the management's internal guidance, and the rise in key margins (GP, EBIT, net profit) led to a ROE rise of 4.4%.

The segment that houses AI accelerators for Nvidia (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) - TSMC's HPC unit - now accounts for 57% of the company's consolidated sales structure. The "insane" demand, which is the exact adjective that the CEO used on the latest earnings call, is what's driving the margin expansion across the board, and it doesn't look to be topping just yet.

The AI demand actually continue to be very strong, it's more -- more stronger than we thought 3 months ago, okay? So in today's situation, we have talked to customers and then we talk to customers' customer.

So the CAGR we -- previously we announced is about mid-40s. It's still -- it's a little bit better than that. We will update you probably in beginning of next year. So we have a more clear picture. Today, the number are insane.

Source: TSMC's Q3 earnings call.

TSMC's offerings are best-in-class, helping the company secure the largest share within the pure foundry market right now, and what's noteworthy - TSMC's share there keeps expanding further.

What doesn't let others match TSMC's scale in the advanced node market (where the massive profits lie) is the capital spending. TSMC narrowed its FY2025 CAPEX plan, but it still guided for $40-42 billion, expecting to scale toward $50 billion in 2026/2027. Samsung (SSNLF) and Intel Corp. (INTC) spend ~$36.3 billion and $16.9 billion in CAPEX on a TTM basis, based on Seeking Alpha data, and these are diversified companies, so only a little fraction of these sums goes to their foundries. This unmatched scale is one of the main catalysts for 2026 - I expect another 1-2% addition to TSMC's market share, and its near-monopolistic earnings expansion that should follow the general upward-sloping trend in AI spending.

But the case for TSMC's superiority isn't just about its spending and operationally expanding moat. The second major driver for 2026 that I see is TSMC's continuing transition to the 2-nanometer node. Currently, the 3nm mode contributes 23% in sales (as of Q3 data), which is up from virtually nothing a year ago. It's a higher-margin product for TSMC, and it's already fully booked.

But what comes next is 2nm, which is going to be even more favorable for the company's consolidated pricing and margins.

If 2nm is on track for volume production in late 2025, it means that it's going to hit the P&L in a meaningful way in fiscal 2026. That's why over the past 3-6 months, we've seen only bullish annual EPS revisions:

Plus, we know that TSMC introduced A16, a 1.6nm tech that features "Super Power Rail" (backside power delivery), and it's scheduled for H2 2026, so for FY2027, the EPS projections are likely to be lifted again (assuming 1.6 nm is priced higher, again). That's the second catalyst - the forward EPS revisions will likely drive the stock price higher as the market starts to reflect the positives in advance.

One more catalyst for TSMC's expansion in 2026 that I see relates to the "Foundry 2.0." The firm's CoWoS (Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrate), which is a proprietary tech that enables stitches together logic chips and HBM (High Bandwidth Memory) into a single powerful unit, is what can expand TSMC's total addressable market meaningfully. Now TSMC's peers, like the same Intel and Samsung, must replicate TSMC's specific packaging yields and thermal management capabilities to compete more effectively, and I simply can't see how they can achieve that given their capital spending. The management revealed that advanced packaging revenue is approaching ~10% of total revenue and growing faster than the corporate average, so I see a massive high-margin potential on that front in 2026-2027.

The last catalyst that I see for the medium term, and it might sound a bit illogical in the bullish terms, is related to geopolitics. Just a few days ago, on December 23rd, the U.S. announced new tariffs on Chinese-made chips starting in June 2027. It's actually a massive tailwind for TSMC as it continues to invest in Arizona (TSMC pledged >$100 billion in investment for its Arizona site). Their first fab is already ramping up 4nm production, and there should come more in the future.

This should effectively price out legacy chips from SMIC and other Chinese foundries for any product destined for the U.S. market.

When I take all the above catalysts together and then take a look at TSMC's valuation picture, I can't call the stock overvalued (although the FWD P/E ratio is approaching the top of the 10-year range):

With systematically higher margins, assuming the current peaks are there to stay with us for the next 2-3 years as the AI-related demand remains hot, I believe TSMC will keep its P/E ratio within the 28-31x range.

Over the past 4 years, TSMC has beaten the consensus by 0.5-1.0%, so I'll take the midpoint of that range as a target premium for the FY2026 EPS assumption.

As a result, TSMC's fair value per share arrives at ~$378 with a 12-month horizon.

$12.72 * 1.0075 * 29.5x = $378.05/share (+26% upside).

Where Can I Be Wrong?

One of the biggest risks remains geopolitics. I treat this factor in a positive way when discussing TSMC's prospects, but in reality, we're still facing a risk of a full-scale invasion of China into Taiwan. It's probably the worst-case scenario, but all investors should constantly have this factor on their radar and monitor the situation carefully.

Also, if there's an overcapacity or if the AI hype cycle bursts in 2026/27, TSM will be left with massive depreciation costs from its pre-planned $50 billion CAPEX plan. Today's demand side doesn't indicate any such risks in the medium term, but it's just my view on the current situation. I may be wrong in extrapolating the same trends to 2026 and beyond.

TSMC is also not dirt cheap. In other words, it's only cheap if certain conditions - like the catalysts that I described today - materialize shortly. If TSM stock's P/E drops to 25x and its EPS comes in with a discount of 5% to the current FY2026 consensus, the stock's upside potential turns to 1-2%. That's not enough margin of safety, in my view.

The Bottom Line

I believe that TSMC may be the only company that is capable of manufacturing the "brains" of this new AI infrastructure at scale, and its most recent financials easily prove that. The upcoming 2026 should be prolific when it comes to new catalysts. My valuation model says that TSMC stock is probably 26%+ undervalued after its 2024-2025 run-up. That's why I leave my Buy unchanged.

Thank you for reading!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Iron Mountain: Strong Core Operations And Fresh Value
data.description:
Iron Mountain Incorporated (IRM) has become a bit of a battleground stock. Bulls point toward rapid growth and a low AFFO multiple, while bears are concerned by high debt and AFFO calculations.

The truth, as usual, is somewhere in the middle.

This article will address some of the most debated aspects of Iron Mountain and try to provide a clean perspective on valuation, balance sheet, and growth.

Let us start by examining the bear thesis, as it has clearly had quite a bit of traction with regard to IRM’s stock price.

The IRM Bear Thesis:

I think there are 3 reasons IRM’s stock price has dropped substantially in the last year.

  1. IRM got too expensive.
  2. It got caught in the AI selloff.
  3. Short attack.

Iron Mountain is a complex business dealing with all forms of information management. Decades ago, this primarily meant physical boxes of paper that IRM would store securely in vaults.

As information usage evolved, IRM evolved with it. IRM now converts physical information to digital and is one of the world’s leading companies at managing, securing, and retrieving both physical and digital information.

Part of that digital transformation involves the building of data centers in which to store and utilize said information on behalf of its clients. In the process, the market started trading IRM as a data center REIT.

While IRM does have an active data center pipeline, it is still a relatively small portion of their business. Thus, I thought it became overvalued as it got swept up in AI/data center excitement and is now coming back down with the AI bubble partially deflating toward the end of 2025.

We will dig deeper into valuation later in this article, but with the selloff, it is starting to look quite a bit more reasonable.

The most recent leg down in IRM’s stock price seemed to be related to a short attack.

In my opinion, each point emphasized by the bear thesis can be fairly easily disproven.

Regarding leverage, a significant portion of the discrepancy is related to operating lease liability, highlighted in yellow below.

This is $2.28B of liability that exists on the balance sheet, but it is not really debt. IRM rents a portion of their buildings, which, starting in 2016 with ASU 2016-02, gets recorded as a liability for the entire amount owned under the lease.

It’s not really debt. Sure, IRM will have to pay the rent, but it is over a very long period of time, and in exchange, they will get to use the properties and generate revenues from the properties.

  • $2B of debt is a net present value of negative $2B.
  • $2B of lease liability has a net present value of approximately 0.

Thus, IRM excludes this liability from their debt calculation, which I think they are right to do, and it is fairly common practice to do so.

IRM’s calculations of debt to EBITDA are essentially verified by the free market. In September, they raised 1.2 billion Euros at 4.75%, which is a rate that implies a healthy credit profile.

Finally, the other matter that seems to be bothering bears is the idea that IRM’s reported AFFO is inflated.

In this case, I agree with bears in direction, but it is something for which we can easily correct as IRM provides clear reconciliation.

Reported earnings are often inflated relative to true earnings. The fact of the matter is that GAAP accounting is not perfect at quantifying earnings in varying lines of business. Thus, companies use non-GAAP metrics to present earnings in a way that makes more sense for their specific business type.

S&P companies often use adjusted earnings per share.

REITs, like IRM, use Adjusted Funds From Operations or AFFO.

Companies are, of course, going to use adjustments that present earnings favorably. In my experience, the vast majority of companies present reported adjusted earnings that are higher than “true” earnings.

The key is that they do so in a transparent way. IRM’s adjustments are completely transparent.

In each earnings report, they concurrently submit SEC filings with a full reconciliation of non-GAAP metrics to GAAP metrics. Any investor can access this reconciliation for free and see exactly what is being added and deducted from GAAP numbers.

In going from GAAP income to normalized FFO, there are 2 large line items which have received some scrutiny (highlighted below).

I am entirely fine with the restructuring add-back because it is a one-time expense, and AFFO is meant to be a sort of run-rate figure. The restructuring charges are related to project Matterhorn, which involved quite a bit of leaning out of operations. In so doing, it incurred employee severance fees and other costs to clean up operations.

These charges are ending in 4Q25 as discussed by Barry Hytinen, IRM’s CFO, at the 12/11/25 conference:

“This is the last quarter of Matterhorn restructuring, and we don't have any additional restructuring dollars. And just to kind of answer a question that might be out there, those were onetime in nature. So you should not be anticipating additional of that sort going forward. And in fact, what you should be expecting to see is significantly incremental free cash flow, which we will use to further grow our business and/or need relatively less debt for the same line of growth.”

With 4Q25 being the last quarter of restructuring charges, I think they qualify as one-time and are correctly added back to AFFO.

Stock-based comp, however, is a recurring cost and a real cost even if it is non-cash. Thus, in calculating true AFFO, I would subtract approximately $120 million from the annual reported figure.

IRM also reconciles the adjustments from normalized FFO to AFFO.

Non-real estate depreciation is a tricky line item. While real estate depreciation is and should be added back for REITs due to buildings usually holding their value, it is less clear to me that the same would hold true for non-real estate.

Much of IRM’s non-real estate depreciation is related to its large fleet of vehicles, which do indeed lose value over time. I think the pace of true value loss is a bit slower than the pace at which they depreciate in accounting terms. As such, I would count this as a $200 million deduction to AFFO ($50 million quarterly) from IRM’s reported figure.

Adding this to the stock-based comp add-back, I calculate IRM’s true AFFO as $320 million lower than reported AFFO. Divided over 295.6 million shares, that is $1.08 lower AFFO/share.

So, IRM’s guided AFFO/share is $5.08 at the midpoint.

True AFFO/share by my calculation is $4.00 in 2025. We will discuss this further in the valuation section.

The IRM Bull Case

Iron Mountain has put together a strong track record of growth coming from a variety of business segments.

Even though the RIM business (records information management) has been considered a legacy paper business, IRM has impressive pricing power and is able to significantly increase the prices it charges each year. This has led to it being as much of a growth engine as the data center builds. NOI for IRM has improved rapidly and consistently.

While some share issuance was involved in the capital outlay, the rates of return were sufficiently high that it was accretive on a per share basis. IRM’s data center builds have cash on cash IRRs in the low double digits, which easily overcome IRM’s cost of capital.

Thus, the external growth has led to healthy AFFO/share growth.

Analysts are projecting similarly strong growth through 2028.

I largely agree with the consensus analysts, at least directionally.

Since IRM’s data center builds have a contract in place before even being built, the returns are largely derisked.

The information management business also looks ironclad, as data retrieval is integral to clients. It is also quite cumbersome to switch providers, which gives IRM continued pricing power.

My biggest gripe about the bull case and analyst forecasts is that they aren’t reconciled to true AFFO, which has implications for valuation.

IRM Valuation

At face value, IRM is trading at 14X forward AFFO and 16X trailing AFFO.

With IRM’s growth rate, that multiple would be extremely undervalued.

Recall, however, that sell-side analysts are incented to accurately predict AFFO. If they forecast 2026 AFFO/share of $5.71 and then IRM reports $5.71, they look prescient, and it helps their businesses.

This means that analysts typically use the same calculations as the company’s reported figures.

Thus, we should adjust forecasted AFFO numbers in the same way we adjusted IRM’s reported AFFO to “true” AFFO.

The figures we adjusted, stock comp and non-real estate depreciation, are largely recurring and flat. As such, I would take a similar $1.08 off the AFFO/share forecasts going forward. This takes, 2026 AFFO/share to $4.63, which implies a forward multiple of 17.23X.

Overall, I think the selloff in combination with IRM’s growth has taken IRM to a reasonably attractive valuation. While I would be hesitant to call Equinix (EQIX) and Digital Realty (DLR) peers since IRM is only partially a data center, Iron Mountain’s multiple is substantially lower.

IRM’s information management business is more akin to the IT sector, and indeed IRM appears to be valued cheaper than most of those companies, although I will admit to being less well versed in IT as it is outside of my wheelhouse.

A 17X true AFFO multiple is an attractive valuation for a company growing at about 11% with a 4.4% dividend yield. As long as the fundamentals hold up, it should be a recipe for outperformance.

With that in mind, let us look at a few risks that could derail the fundamentals.

Risks To IRM

It has long been speculated that paper storage volumes would decline. I think there is some merit to the concept, as paper usage has certainly declined. The outcome for IRM will be a matter of how effectively they can transition paper storage to full digital, including picking up new storage volume directly as digital.

So far, IRM seems to be executing well on that pathway and is actually at the highest storage volume they have ever had.

There is some risk in mix shift. Revenue per volume is much higher in the U.S., while their storage volume is growing faster outside the U.S. If this trend continues, it could cause some margin compression. So far, they have been able to offset and actually grow margins by raising prices. Going forward, it will be a matter of whether price increases or mix shift winds up being a bigger factor.

On the data center side of the business, I see minimal risk to existing revenues, as they are mostly contracts with extremely high-credit tenants. The risk instead would be to future growth. Data center demand is hot right now, but in future scenarios where data centers get overbuilt, incremental leasing demand could drop off.

The Bottom Line

While adjustments should be made to IRM’s AFFO to get a more accurate view of valuation, their operations are clean. IRM operates effectively and transparently. Given the recent drop in stock price, IRM is now trading at an opportunistic valuation relative to their high-growth rate.

We are long IRM.

data.title: CLOU: A Meticulously Constructed Cloud Computing ETF
data.description:
Introduction to the Global X Cloud Computing ETF

The Global X Cloud Computing ETF (CLOU) is an ETF with $273 million AUM by Global X Management Company LLC, which is a subsidiary of the Mirae Asset Financial Group (a South Korea based financial services entity). CLOU, which began trading on the markets in April 2019 can be accessed at an expense ratio of 0.68%. This ETF does not pay any dividends.

What Does CLOU Do, and How Is It Built?

CLOU’s goal is to provide exposure to global stocks that could profit from the increasing penetration of cloud computing technology. Put another way, this portfolio covers businesses that are increasingly benefiting from the trend of computing resources (software, servers, storage, etc.) being tapped remotely (over the internet) and on-demand, rather than through on-premises or local infrastructure.

CLOU does not actively pick its portfolio of cloud computing stocks, but rather replicates the Indxx Global Cloud Computing Index [IGCCI], which in turn employs multiple screeners to arrive at its final coverage.

IGCCI covers stocks from both developed markets and emerging markets, but makes sure not to include any stocks from India, Taiwan and Vietnam. IGCCI also requires its stocks to have a float percentage (shares that can be freely traded) that accounts for at least 10% of the total shares outstanding, and average turnover of at least $2M per day over a 6-month time frame. These stocks should also have a minimum market-cap of at least $200 million.

Once a stock passes these initial screeners, it needs to score highly on the cloud computing front. What we mean is that any potential stock should generate “at least half” their revenue from “cloud computing”, or at least $500 million in revenue from “public cloud infrastructure”. Investors should note that the former category (cloud computing) is quite expansive and can have a number of sub-categories under it. These categories include:

  • SaaS stocks (Software as a service) that generate recurring revenue or subscription revenue through cloud-hosted software,
  • PaaS stocks (Platform as a service) that provide cloud-based platforms for software development and deployment,
  • IaaS stocks (Infrastructure as a service) that provide computing resources like hardware, storage, etc. through the cloud,
  • Data-center REITs, which are not cloud computing businesses in the traditional sense, but own and manage secure data storage facilities,
  • Cloud and edge computing infrastructure providers that are manufacturers and distributors of hardware for cloud and edge computing.

Once the 50% revenue exposure is confirmed, the top 30 companies with the the greatest exposure to cloud computing are chosen, alongside the top 10 companies with the greatest exposure to public cloud infrastructure (if there are fewer than 10, then all stocks from this public cloud category get included). IGCCI also seeks to keep its exposure to data-center REITs limited to just 5 stocks.

As far as portfolio weighting is concerned (adjustments are made twice a year in May and November), traditional cloud computing stocks get a maximum weight of 4%, while public cloud companies receive a lower weight of 2%. Also note that the aggregate weight of all public cloud companies and the aggregate weight of all data-center REITs is capped at 10% each, which suggests that 80% of the portfolio will be oriented around traditional cloud computing stocks.

What Are the Standout Traits of CLOU’s Portfolio?

Firstly consider that CLOU does not have a very broad portfolio, and only focuses on 38 stocks in total. Normally when an ETF covers such a small pool of stocks, one is bound to see strong concentration effects, or an inherent top-heaviness, but CLOU’s top 10 stocks account for a little over 40% of the portfolio, which is pretty much in line with the top 10 stake of most ETFs.

With regards to industry exposure, given the weighting emphasis placed on core cloud computing stocks (as opposed to REITs, etc.), it is no surprise to discover that an overwhelming share of this portfolio (88%) comes from software & services stocks.

In terms of regional exposure, even though this is supposed to be a global portfolio, it's concentrated in North America (93% of the portfolio), with stocks based in the US alone taking up the lion’s share (87.4%).

From a market-cap perspective, CLOU tilts more towards small-caps (41% of the portfolio) than any other market-cap bracket. Micro-cap exposure too could potentially have been a lot more, but do recollect that during the screening process, CLOU’s tracking index rejects any stock that does not have a minimum market-cap of $200 million (so only the micro-caps from $200M-$300 million are considered).

Stylistically, there’s little surprise to discover that this cloud computing portfolio largely tilts towards growth-style stocks (almost half the portfolio), with hybrid stocks too having a decent share (36%). This means this portfolio is likely to have stocks with high momentum quotients, strong sales and earnings growth profiles, but pricey valuations and low payouts.

What Are the Risks Associated With CLOU?

CLOU isn’t meant for those who dislike seeing wide swings in the movements of their portfolio over a course of the year. Take note of CLOU’s annualized volatility profile which is approaching 30%, and 1.7x more than the volatility that most ETFs witness.

In addition to that, CLOU also comes across as a levered bet on the S&P500, so if the prospects of the US benchmark don’t look great, this is probably not a product you want to get too optimistic about. CLOU’s beta of 1.36x versus the S&P500 suggests that if the market drops by 5%, CLOU could fall by a greater margin of closer to 7%.

At an expense ratio of 0.68%, CLOU is not a particularly cheap ETF to own, when you consider that ETFs in general are priced at levels of around 0.5%. Even within the cloud computing space, there are cheaper alternatives priced at expense ratios of 0.35% to 0.6%.

CLOU’s valuations too are a concern at an earnings multiple of 25x, and book value of almost 4x. If CLOU’s holdings aren’t able to execute successfully and their earnings and book value don’t grow fast enough in the future, they could be susceptible to quick and brutal sell-offs given the elevated expectations associated with them (high multiples are a proxy of high market expectations).

Who Is CLOU ETF For?

CLOU is meant for growth-chasing investors who can stomach high volatility and aren’t perturbed by high valuations. The growth potential of companies involved in the cloud computing space looks very enticing through this decade, as a meaningful chunk of enterprise workloads are still on physical servers and haven’t yet moved to the cloud. Reports suggest that the addressable market could expand by 22%, with cloud computing sales expected to cross the $2 trillion barrier by the end of the decade. CLOU as we know isn’t an ETF that focuses on just one category of cloud computing stocks, and this is just as well because all the major categories of cloud computing (SaaS, PaaS, and IAAS) are all expected to have meaningful double-digit shares of the overall address market over time. It also helps that CLOU covers stocks from all major market-cap categories with small-cap stocks (who arguably have the best growth profile) taking up the largest share right now.

CLOU may also represent a useful bet for those who want to indirectly bet on the growing AI wave without owning some of the direct AI proxies that are priced at exorbitant valuations. The growing proliferation of AI is bound to prompt a spurt in data that will need to be managed with even more cloud computing resources.

What Are the Alternatives to CLOU?

Investors who are interested in exploring other ETF options that focus on cloud computing stocks aren’t hard-pressed for choice, as there are close to a dozen of other alternatives. Within that bracket, we’re also highlighting the profiles of the First Trust Cloud Computing ETF (SKYY), and the Fidelity Cloud Computing ETF (FLCD) both of which are also passively managed but track two different indices from CLOU.

Investors may also want to consider that since FLCD (the product with the shortest listing history) got listed, our ETF in focus-CLOU has lost ground, even as these two alternatives have moved ahead, while having a tug-of-war amongst themselves for AUM.

SKYY is the oldest and largest ETF to cover cloud computing stocks; it’s been around for over 14 years now, during which it has garnered AUM that is 11x more than our ETF in focus (incidentally as of 2025, it is the only pure-play cloud computing ETF to cross the billion-dollar landmark). SKYY also covers the widest pool of stocks (66), with a more balanced spread between different market-cap brackets. Note that SKYY doesn’t come across as being too top-heavy with a top 10 weight of 35%. This is a function of its popularity, but its bid-ask spreads too are smaller compared the other two, suggesting that short-term tactical players have a higher probability of getting more favorable price entries and exits with SKYY than the other two. A drawback of SKYY is that it has stopped making quarterly distributions since 2022.

FLCD stands out from the other two because of its comparatively cheaper cost profile (an expense ratio of 0.39%), and also because of its strong tilt towards mid-cap cloud computing stocks (which have better growth profiles than their larger peers, and are not as risky as their small and micro-cap peers). Investors may also be interested to know that FCLD also makes compensatory quarterly distributions, although admittedly they don’t annualize to a compelling yield at current prices. FCLD is also not overly over-exposed to software and IT services stocks like our ETF in focus. FCLD’s drawback is that it doesn’t seem to attract a lot of fund flows, and this also translates to pretty wide spreads between its bid and the ask, raising the risk of slippages.

Summary

CLOU focuses on a narrow portfolio of cloud computing stocks (with a small-cap tilt) that are innately quite volatile but also have high growth prospects. CLOU, whose underlying index employs a rigorous screening process during portfolio construction, may also be seen as an indirect way to profit from the AI boom.

This article answers these three main questions about CLOU:

  1. What are the main features of the CLOU portfolio?
  2. What type of investor is CLOU suitable for, and what are the risks associated with it?
  3. How does CLOU compare to other cloud computing ETFs?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Visa Vs. Mastercard: Two Payment Giants, One Clear Favorite For Me
data.description:
Thesis

Visa Inc. (V) and Mastercard Incorporated (MA) share an extraordinarily durable duopoly built on scale, network effects, and secular digital payment growth. Even though Mastercard is growing faster and tends to innovate more, I think Visa is the better buy right now. Overall, Visa has a slight edge on margins, carries less financial baggage (a cleaner balance sheet), and costs less based on its current valuation.

A Scalable Payments Duopoly Built for Long-Term Growth

Visa and Mastercard are the undisputed “gatekeepers” in the card payments world. Two giants running a seamless system that links four key groups: the banks that issue cards, the merchants that accept them, the companies that process the transactions for merchants (acquirers), and the consumers who use the cards. It's an elegant setup, asset-light and brilliantly scalable.

This business model has minted money for years because it often generates operating margins above 60 because fixed costs don’t scale much with volume. Speaking of volume, they're propelled by unstoppable shifts like the vanishing use of cash, e-commerce's ongoing devour of retail, and money zipping across borders faster than ever. And that cash usage, it's no surprise that it's expected to fall to over 40% as this year comes to a close, pushing billions into digital channels dominated by these networks.

That broader economic boost helps explain why both companies are certainly making a lot of money right now. But the differences in how they’re performing point to distinct long-term strategies behind the scenes. Visa draws about 40% of its revenue from the U.S. (tying it tighter to American consumer whims), while Mastercard brings in 55% from international markets (although that is steadily decreasing).

Let’s look at the latest quarterlies for both companies. Revenue-wise, Mastercard went up 17% YoY compared to Visa’s 12%.

Cross-border volumes help explain the reason for the gap; there, again, Mastercard had the edge, with volumes up 15% vs. 12% for Visa. Digging for clues, I found that both in Asia and Europe there was a slight resurgence in travel, and digital adoption has been a lot faster, especially in Asia, than in the West.

Brand-wise, and this is simply my broad overview of each one, I tend to see Visa having an edge on the “trust” side due to its government ties on inclusion projects, such as digitizing aid in developing nations, whereas Mastercard, to me, is more about tech integrations and therefore the bigger innovator between the two. Overall, the small differences between the two companies make their competitive advantages even stronger.

High switching costs keep banks and partners locked in, which makes it hard to imagine a bank tearing out either company’s payment rails and replacing them with a competitor. Also keep in mind that network effects build momentum: as more users join, the networks become more valuable, which attracts even more users.

I'd say that regulations further strengthen their competitive fortress. This year's compliance overhauls, including Visa’s streamlined monitoring programs, raise the cost of entry so much that most fintech challengers, especially popular buy-now-pay-later services, choose to partner rather than compete head-on. Visa’s traction in that space is accelerating, which reinforces the point.

Circling back to the point about Mastercard being more technology-driven than its peer, a core strength shows up in its value-added services. Mastercard leads in this mix, as extras like fraud prevention tools and data insights now account for 40% of its revenue. In terms of growth, that segment saw a 22% increase through September. So by comparison, Visa offers a broader set of similar services, but they grow more slowly and make up 27% of Visa’s total revenue.

In emerging markets, they are expanding aggressively with governments in Latin America are sending public aid digitally through their payment networks, and Visa’s partnerships helped distribute assistance to millions of people last quarter. At the same time, digital wallets are growing rapidly. Mastercard’s partnership with Tencent (TCEHY) embeds payments directly into entertainment apps across China. Cryptocurrency remains a side business. But it still packs weight. Visa, for instance, processed more than $225 million in stablecoin settlements this year without really taking on full crypto exposure.

Stress-Tested by the Consumer

All this talk about strategy becomes clearest when consumer spending gets put to the test, and the recent holiday shopping season gave us that real-world proof. Overall, shoppers didn't hold back too much, they drove U.S. retail sales up 4.2% from November 1 to December 21, according to Visa’s tally, with electronics and apparel leading spending, and in-store purchases made up 73% of total sales.

Mastercard data shows a slightly lower increase of 3.9%. It also highlighted that online deals attracted more value-focused shoppers regardless of the paradox that household budgets are still tight. Another key factor to watch is that credit card balances rose in tandem. While this benefits both companies, there is, of course, the flip-side to this trend.

That said, late-stage missed payments edged up modestly the previous quarter, reaching 3.04% for credit cards (but the latest data does has it going down a bit), reminding us that growing debt levels put pressure on the lower-income folks. Still, Visa and Mastercard remain largely protected because they collect fees on transactions without taking on the credit risk that hurts card issuers when customers fall behind on payments.

That protection got a little boost in investor sentiment after the recent ATM antitrust settlement. Visa and Mastercard agreed to pay a combined $167.5 million to resolve claims that they fixed fees at non-bank ATMs, a legal fight that dated back to 2011. Visa paid $88.8 million, and Mastercard paid $78.7 million and both companies settled without admitting any wrongdoing. In the grand scheme of things, I think the outcome looks more like a PR win than a financial setback.

And that brings us back to the issue that never really goes away for Visa and Mastercard. Both companies face the same problem: regulators keep coming back. Just last week, Walmart (WMT) and several retail trade groups criticized the proposed $38 billion swipe-fee settlement as too weak and they argued that a 0.1 percentage point reduction spread over five years does not go far enough and called for steeper cuts. Their push mirrors ongoing backlash from merchants who want stronger card-routing options so they can avoid expensive, rewards-heavy credit cards.

What the Market Is Paying For Each Network

Visa trades at a forward P/E ratio of 27.8x, which is cheaper than Mastercard’s 35.2x.

This gap lines up with their different growth rates, but it also suggests Visa might be the better value (yes, they both get sector "F" grades, I'm merely going with apples to apples comps right now), considering both stocks have lagged the broader market YTD.

Looking at enterprise value to EBITDA, Visa trades at 24.5x on a trailing basis, which is below its five-year average of 25.1x.

Mastercard trades at 27x, also below its own five-year average of 30.3x. Overall, we can see that these figures show that both companies are trading at discounts compared with their historical valuations, and I’d argue that this reflects some of the investor caution toward the broader fintech sector.

Free cash flow multiples mirror much of the same. Visa trades at 23.7x forward free cash flow, while Mastercard trades at a much higher 38.1x.

Presumably the market rewards Visa for its size but penalizes its rival for the greater premium investors pay for stronger foreign exposure.

That said, that valuation gap reflects different growth expectations. Analysts expect Mastercard’s earnings per share to rise by 16.1% in 2026, compared with 12.7% for Visa.

But those higher growth expectations aren’t coming out of thin air. Mastercard’s value-added services grew 22% last quarter, helping drive projected revenue growth of 13.6% year over year.

Visa delivers steadier profit margins above 65% and generates incremental returns on capital that beat most competitors.

Mastercard, however, gains the advantage from faster growth in emerging markets and stronger exposure to foreign exchange trends, which, I'd argue, support its 20–30% valuation premium.

Both Visa and Mastercard have very strong balance sheets. Visa holds an AA- credit rating and keeps debt low at 32% of capital, while Mastercard carries a slightly lower A+ rating and much higher leverage at 71% (see previous graph).

Even so, both companies generate massive free cash flow, Visa produces more than $20 billion a year, and Mastercard brings in close to $18 billion annually.

Shareholder payouts reinforce that financial strength. Visa returned $22.8 billion to shareholders in fiscal 2025 through buybacks and dividends (see historical stats above), including a 14% dividend increase to $0.67 per quarter.

Mastercard approved a a new $14 billion share-repurchase program and matched that with its own 14% dividend increase (also see stats above).

Final Takeaway

I would rate Visa a Strong Buy and Mastercard a Buy. Mastercard is growing faster, but investors are paying a slightly higher price for that growth, and it also carries more debt. Visa currently has the best risk-reward ratio. Overall, Visa has comparable long-term payment network benefits, but marginally better profit margins, a stronger financial sheet, and a lower price multiple.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Walmart: This Is A 2024 Costco Situation
data.description:
From mid-2022 to today, Walmart Inc.'s (WMT) stock essentially moved in only one direction. Similar to Costco (COST), Walmart has now reached a point where it trades far above what I view as its fundamental floor.

For so long, investors who bet against Costco turned out to be foolish, as the company's P/E multiple continued to expand to levels beyond anybody's imagination. Eventually, though, stock performance that's entirely based on multiple expansion dries out.

Costco has been a significant underperformer over the past X, and I argue Walmart's fortunes could very well be similar from current levels.

Walmart's Remarkable Run

For the better part of a decade, Walmart was perceived as a boring, slow-growing stock that may only be relevant for low-risk portfolios that are looking to preserve capital, rather than beat the market.

Indeed, from May 2012 to May 2022, the market yielded returns twice as high as Walmart.

Then, Walmart finally turned a new leaf. After years of under-the-radar investment and strategic planning, inflation pushed price-conscious shoppers into Walmart's arms. What they found was an improved shopping experience, everyday low prices, an attractive membership plan, and by far the best online offering among traditional brick & mortar retailers.

The once-boring and slow behemoth has turned into an exciting omnichannel story, which includes recurring revenues from memberships, high-margin businesses like advertising, and a market share gains trajectory.

There's one thing you can be sure of: this market loves a physical moat business. Very quickly, bears became bulls, and Walmart's stock has soared nearly 3x since mid-2022, more than 2.3x the market.

Results Continue To Be Strong, But Not Extraordinary

Pretty much since 2022, every quarter for Walmart has been roughly the same, and that's not a bad thing. Like clockwork, marginally beating expectations, with 4%-6% revenue growth, on top of 6%-10% profit growth.

Last quarter was no different. Total revenue was $179.5 billion, up 5.8% Y/Y, or 6.0% on a constant currency basis, driven by a 27% increase in global ecommerce sales and a 17% increase in membership fee income.

Gross profit improved slightly by 2 bps to 24.2%, while adjusted operating expenses as a percentage of sales improved by 4 bps to 21.1%. Combined with revenue growth, this yielded an 8% increase in adjusted operating income.

On a segment level, Walmart U.S maintained comp sales of 4.5%, driven by a 1.8% increase in transactions and a 2.7% increase in average ticket. Total sales were up 5.1%, with another quarter of ecommerce growth above 20%. Operating income rose 6.3%.

Walmart International sales grew 10.8% to $33.5 billion, or 11.4% on constant currency, including a 26% increase in ecommerce led by India, China, and Mexico. Adjusted operating income improved 16.9% to $1.4 billion, fueled by strong advertising growth.

Sam's Club revenue was $23.6 billion, up 3.1% Y/Y. Comp sales excluding fuel prices were up 3.8%, with transactions up 3.9% and average ticket down 0.1%. E-commerce sales rose 22%, and membership income grew 7.1%. Operating income was up 5.8% to $0.7 billion.

All up, this was a typical Walmart quarter. It brought nothing new to the table, but it showcased the extreme consistency investors expected.

Building on that notion, Walmart itself hasn't changed its outlook too meaningfully. Net sales growth was raised, while the profit outlook was narrowed upwards.

Walmart Trades Far Above Its Fundamental Floor

In my view, a good framework for evaluating the attractiveness of a stock is a "Fundamental Floor" concept. In a nutshell, a fundamental floor is based on the company's earnings, growth, and quality, and seeks to answer the following question:

At what point, does the stock become a no-brainer?

For Peter Lynch, this was simply a 1x price to earnings to growth ratio, or PEG. Today, this 1x multiple is unrealistic. Most good companies trade above 1.5x, and very good companies tend to average around 2x.

At $111 per share, Walmart is far from a no-brainer stock, and far from the 2x PEG ratio many high-quality growth companies trade at.

At 42x forward earnings and 37x next year's estimates, on long-term growth expectations of 11%-12%, Walmart trades above a 3x PEG. For a company with mid-single-digit revenue growth and a commitment to low prices, there's not much upside surprises to that 11%-12% trajectory either.

Meanwhile, a retailer like Amazon (AMZN), trading at 30x earnings, with earnings growth expectations around 20%, trades at a 1.5x PEG, which is 50% lower compared to Walmart.

Is it fair to say that Walmart's fundamental floor is at least a 2x PEG, which translates into a 25x P/E? I think it very much is. In fact, this is far higher than where the stock used to trade just two years ago, as shown on the graph.

Again, I'm not saying Walmart should necessarily trade at 25 times earnings, I'm just saying that the stock could fall more than 25%, and it still won't reach a "No-Brainer Valuation."

Resemblance To 2024 Costco

The Costco valuation debate is one of the most popular ones among investors. On one hand, there are those who argue that there has to be a limit to a company's valuation, regardless of how great it is. On the other hand, there are those who say that this was true for Costco at 30x earnings, then it reached 50x, so why should they think it's not going to 60x earnings?

The issue I have with the second argument is that it's based on other investors' willingness to buy a stock at a higher price. It isn't based on the company, its results, or its growth prospects. At that point, you might as well buy Bitcoin.

For me, it's one thing if you bought a stock when it was attractive and kept holding even when the valuation got too high, perhaps out of loyalty, wanting to avoid taxes, or simply because you have a rule of never selling your winners.

There's a big difference if you bought the stock when the valuation was already too high, and you were hoping it would get even higher.

Indeed, over the past two years, investors were no longer willing to bid up Costco, and the stock turned into a laggard. While I presume those who have been holding the stock for a decade don't really care, I'm certain that those who bought at 60x earnings feel quite foolish.

Conclusion

In my mind, the best case scenario for Walmart at the current valuation is that it will maintain it. In other words, I don't see room for further multiple expansion, or more accurately, I don't see justification for further expansion. We can never know what the market will do.

This means that investors should expect, at the very best case, 10%-12% annual returns, but that comes with a huge amount of risk. For example, if Walmart's multiple contracts to 35x, that would erase two years of returns.

I was right here predicting that Walmart would reach a 40x multiple one day, but now that it surpassed it, I believe it has reached the point investors should Hold or trim, definitely not buy.

data.title: Harbour Energy: Another Acquisition
data.description:
Harbour Energy (PMOIF) is an English company that reports using American dollars. As an aside, remember that English companies typically report every six months. The last article mentioned how much progress has been made by straightening out the balance sheet from years past. It could be that there is a turnaround underway. Now the company has made its biggest bet yet by entering the Gulf of America by way of an acquisition, where a whole lot of United States production is located. If this works out, it could prove to be extremely profitable for investors.

Acquisition

The company is acquiring a privately held business for a combination of cash and common stock. The transaction is expected to keep the company as an investment grade idea. Keep in mind that few overseas ideas are investment grade. This is often a way to stay away from some of the ideas that are meant to separate investors from their money.

For starters, this gets the company into a lower tax area. Compared to some of the places in which it operates, this is a breath of fresh air.

This is in addition to an announcement of a small second acquisition from Waldorf Energy Partners and Waldorf (currently in administration) for roughly $170 million. This acquisition essentially increases the interest in properties that the company currently operates. A successful acquisition means the release of $350 million being held which essentially pays for the transaction (and then some). Note that there is also a creditor claim situation which needs to be resolved and might cost some money (but then again may not depending upon how things go). The expectation shown below is to gain control of the excess cash (for corporate purposes). This deal will close more slowly (probably in the upcoming second quarter).

Continuation Of Transactions

There is also a sale. When that is combined with the two deals previously discussed there is a cash inflow of roughly $400 million from the sale and the Waldorf deal expectations to help finance the large transaction.

The inflow of cash combined with the value of stock used represents roughly one-third of the transaction value. This is the kind of thing that good managements seem to be able to do all the time to keep the company leverage where they want it to be.

Based upon what was covered before, the summary shown above should be no surprise. The debt ratio will rise but it will remain just above 1.0 after the completion of all the transactions.

This management has in the past kept the debt ratio way below one so that if a deal like this came along, then the company could handle it while remaining investment grade. Therefore, investors may expect that it is very likely management will find a way to bring the debt ratio down yet again for more flexibility while going forward.

This is easily the largest transaction to date. It is also a complementary transaction in that the company did not have a presence in the Gulf anything close to what it will have after the transaction closes. Therefore, the accretive nature of the deal comes largely from the financing leverage.

There is also benefits here in that any United States business is likely to be taxed at a far lower rate than is the case for British business (and hence the average overall tax rate).

The Business

Currently, the company is known for its North Sea operations (though that is not the only area in which the company operates). This acquisition adds a growing business to the portfolio with a fair amount of growth potential in the future.

One of the things that is likely to lower the risk of this acquisition is that the management team in place is highly likely to be retained. This is a business that the existing management needs to learn all about before they even think of trying to actively manage the situation. But that means to expect some sort of decentralized structure to the company going forward.

Anything that would cause a "brain drain" to this critical acquisition consideration is likely high on the avoidance list.

Summary

The reasons for the acquisition are shown below:

One of the things to note for investors is the initiation of a stock buyback program. This actually helps to defend the dividend because the buyback program is easier to discontinue (because there is less blowback from discontinuing it). That potential discontinuation provides a cushion before it's time to consider cutting the dividend. In some ways, this is a very American strategy that is not often seen overseas.

This strong buy idea has a very long history of acquisitions, as shown below:

Now that the company is investment grade, it has come a very long way from the startup days about a decade or so ago. The action here still means that it is cheaper to grow through acquisitions than to grow organically (faster too).

However, this company is fast getting to the point where organic growth could become material in the future as well.

The actions here coincide with a lot of companies I follow that would seem to indicate that purchase prices for acquisitions are low. There are a fair number of companies doing exactly what this company is doing.

If an eventual sale of the company is expected, I would expect that at a time when acquisition prices are a good deal higher than is the case now. The other possibility is a combination with another undervalued firm in order to achieve more efficiency pricing of the combined company's stock when compared to future prospects. But none of this seems to be in the immediate future. However, I would consider holding a company like this until one of these "sale" type actions happens.

This company has improved tremendously compared to the situation when I first began following it. I would expect continued superior performance to continue.

Risks

This is the largest acquisition that the company has made so far. Larger acquisitions are riskier than smaller acquisitions. So there exists a significant risk of the acquisition failing to meet management expectations.

Post acquisition, this is a fairly decent sized company. However, the offshore business often involves partnerships to minimize the risk of large projects. Partnerships can fail to function properly which leads to a need to reorganize the partnership in a way that excludes one or more partners. With a large acquisition like this one, such an event can set back the future of the company.

There is some political uncertainty marked by threats of tariffs and trade wars. This bargaining needs to be replaced with something that is constructive and permanent. Otherwise, the whole situation could bring on a recession or worse.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SPXS: A -3x Inverse Leveraged Strategy On The S&P 500
data.description:
The Direxion Daily S&P 500® Bear 3X Shares ETF (SPXS) is an inverse leveraged exchange-traded fund designed to provide -3x the daily performance to the S&P 500 (SPX). SPXS is highly liquid with $372 million in net assets with an average of $250mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis, making this strategy ideal for traders seeking to amplify risk. Given the heightened risks involved in trading inverse leveraged strategies, SPXS should only be traded for daily exposure as a long-term position may result in significant value decay and unsalvageable losses.

About Direxion Daily S&P 500® Bear 3X Shares ETF

SPXS was launched on November 5, 2008 by Direxion NYSE Arca Exchange. The strategy utilizes index swap positions to gain the appropriate exposure to provide -3x the daily performance over the SPX. SPXS has an expense ratio of 102bps, making the ETF somewhat more expensive than peer ProShares UltraPro Short S&P 500 ETF (SPXU). Each strategy exhibits substantial liquidity and virtually equivalent performance before fees.

Unlike the Direxion Daily S&P 500 Bull 3X Shares ETF (SPXL), which holds a combination of SPX constituents and swap derivatives, SPXS solely holds index swaps along with the necessary cash collateral.

Index swaps are designed to provide exposure to the performance of an index in exchange for a fixed rate. The two parties that participate in index swap contracts are the payer and receiver. The payer is responsible for paying the receiver the daily performance of the underlying Index. In turn, the receiver pays a fixed rate to the payer in exchange for performance exposure. Performance is settled at the end of each trading day in cash with performance reset on the following trading day.

This is reflected in the performance of SPXS, in which the ETF’s -3x exposure target can only be reflected during a single trading day; holding SPXS for longer than a single trading day can result in a compounding effect that can amplify gains or losses beyond the indicated -3x daily performance target to the SPX.

Inverse strategies present substantially greater risk over standard leveraged strategies as the market is generally expected to grow over the long run as a result of multiple factors like earnings growth and top-grading constituents. Though there may be short-term opportunities to exploit large downward moves in the SPX, the Index is generally expected to grow over time, resulting in long-term downward pressure to SPXS.

As a result of the long-term trends towards $0, SPXS has undergone a number of share splits over the years in order to sustain the share price. This makes a buy-and-hold strategy in SPXS exceptionally risky, even if a market decline is expected. Though the market may turn unexpectedly as a result of a lackluster earnings call by one of the top constituents or a worse-than-expected (or in some cases, better-than-expected) economic print, I believe the average investor and trader may experience a better, long-term outcome by generally being cautiously bullish of the market rather than attempting to time a bear market.

Investor Suitability

SPXS should only be used by experienced traders and should not be considered as a long-term investment. SPXS exhibits substantially greater risk as an inverse leveraged strategy and may result in substantial losses if misused. SPXS should only be used for daily exposure; holding the ETF longer than a single day may result in compounded returns beyond the intended -3x daily performance target as well as value decay and unsalvageable losses.

Additional Risks

There is no guarantee that SPXS will provide -3x the performance of the SPX; multiple factors may influence the price of SPXS, including trading volumes, volatility, and the settlement value of the derivatives. Investors must consider all fees and short-term capital gains taxes as part of their trading strategy; SPXS has a 102bps expense ratio and has an average bid/ask spread of 2bps. Given that SPXS should be traded daily, investors may be exposed to higher tax on capital gains. I believe traders must adhere to strict risk management policies when trading leveraged strategies and must maintain strict sell discipline.

You can review additional risks here and the SEC bulletin here.

Final Thoughts

SPXS was designed to provide traders with -3x the daily performance of the SPX. Given the generally positive direction of the underlying Index, SPXS may trend towards $0/share over time; holding positions in inverse leveraged strategies may result in substantial, unsalvageable losses and should only be considered for daily exposure.

This article answers three main questions about SPSX:

  1. How does SPXS work to produce a -3x daily performance of SPX?
  2. What risks accompany holding SPXS?
  3. How is SPXS intended to be used?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.
